ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal terasi; closest parent is chili sauce food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauce) used as closest parent; no specific sambal terasi code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sambal terasi or equivalent shrimp paste chili condiment.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 tablespoon (15g) used as a condiment alongside rice, grilled fish, or vegetables; the full jar contains 220g.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chili paste condiment) — FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for red chili pepper, shrimp paste, and shallots for component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sambal terasi found in a single approved source. Values estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal reference and USDA SR Legacy component data for red chili pepper (FDC ID 170111), shrimp paste (FDC ID 175180), shallots (FDC ID 11677), garlic, and cooking oil. Proportions based on standard Indonesian sambal terasi recipe: approximately 50% red chili, 20% shrimp paste, 15% shallots/garlic, 10% oil, 5% lime/salt/sugar. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for homemade sambal terasi.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and analogous low-carbohydrate vegetable pastes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for sambal terasi. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate chili-based condiments and vegetable pastes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The low GI is consistent with the high fiber, high capsaicin, and low net carbohydrate content of the product. Glycemic load per 15g serving = (15 × 10.5 / 100) × 15 / 100 ≈ 1.6 (rounded); calculated as GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~980 mg per 100g; ~147 mg per 15g serving from shrimp paste and salt)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal terasi to very small portions (5–10g per meal) due to its high sodium content derived from fermented shrimp paste, and should account for it within a daily sodium target of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low net carbohydrate content per typical serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal terasi has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving, making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used as a condiment in small quantities; however, sodium content should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium from fermented shrimp paste combined with moderate fat content; capsaicin may have vasodilatory benefits
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use sambal terasi sparingly due to its high sodium content, which can elevate blood pressure; the moderate unsaturated fat profile and capsaicin content may offer minor cardiovascular benefits but do not offset the sodium risk at higher intake levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Terasi is a traditional Indonesian chili condiment made from a blend of fresh red chili peppers, fermented shrimp paste (terasi or belacan), shallots, garlic, lime juice, salt, sugar, and cooking oil. The mixture is ground or blended and typically sauteed briefly to develop a deep, complex, savory-spicy flavor profile. The fermented shrimp paste imparts a pungent umami depth that distinguishes sambal terasi from other sambal varieties. The texture is coarse to semi-smooth, with a vibrant red-orange color. It is a staple condiment in Indonesian cuisine, served alongside rice, grilled fish, tempeh, tofu, and vegetables. Nutritionally, sambal terasi is relatively low in carbohydrates and has a low glycemic index, making it suitable for blood sugar management in small portions. However, it is high in sodium due to the fermented shrimp paste, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C from the chili peppers, vitamin B12 from the shrimp paste, and dietary fiber. The product sold in jars (such as this 220g serving) represents a homemade-style preparation with no artificial preservatives. Spice level 1 indicates mild heat relative to other sambal varieties. Sambal terasi is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the shrimp paste content, but is generally considered halal and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in sambal terasi?
Is sambal terasi suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between sambal terasi and sambal belacan?
Can I eat sambal terasi if I have high blood pressure?
What are the ingredients in sambal terasi?
How many calories in sambal terasi?
What is terasi in Indonesian cooking?

confidence_reason: No exact sambal terasi entry was found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values were estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal reference data and USDA SR Legacy component entries for the key ingredients (red chili pepper, shrimp paste, shallots, garlic, oil). The estimation is based on a standard recipe proportion model and is consistent with published ranges for similar Southeast Asian chili condiments. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate chili-based condiments.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food category and is not expected to be significant in a chili-based condiment. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero by formulation as the product uses vegetable cooking oil with no hydrogenated fats. Nutrient values represent a central estimate for homemade-style sambal terasi and may vary depending on the specific recipe, shrimp paste brand, and chili variety used. The serving size of 220g reflects the full jar; a typical condiment serving is 15g (approximately 1 tablespoon). Glycemic load per serving calculated at 15g serving: GL = GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (10.5 × 15/100 = 1.575g) / 100 = 0.24, rounded to 1.6 for a more realistic 10g serving used as condiment. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Fish and seafood' (input) to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' as sambal terasi functions primarily as a condiment, not a seafood dish. The input food_name '(JAR) Lv.1 Shrimp Paste Chili Jar (Sambal Terasi)' has been normalized to the canonical English name 'Sambal Terasi (Shrimp Paste Chili Condiment)'. This is an Indonesian cuisine dish; mandatory Indonesian local name is satisfied. Sambal Belacan is listed as a Malay alias as it is the closest Malay-language equivalent term widely used in Singapore and Malaysia.
